content.text: Operational Readiness in Definition of Done

A feature whose code is correct, prompt is reviewed, and eval suite passes is ready to ship from an engineering perspective. It is not yet operationally ready. Operational readiness is the set of conditions that ensure the feature can be reliably monitored, diagnosed, and recovered in production — by the on-call engineer who was not part of the development team and may encounter the feature for the first time during an incident at 2 AM.

attrs.alt: Five operational readiness pillars: Monitoring, Runbooks, Capacity, Rollback Plan, and Support Docs

content.text: Why Claude Features Have Specific Operational Concerns
Claude integrations introduce operational concerns that standard backend services do not have:
Non-determinism:
the same input can produce different outputs on repeated calls. An alert that fires on one response may not reproduce when the on-call engineer retries the request.
External dependency:
the Anthropic API is a third-party service with its own status, rate limits, and maintenance windows. The on-call engineer needs to distinguish 'Claude is down' from 'our application is broken'.
Token cost as an operational metric:
a code change that accidentally doubles input token size doubles the cost per request. Without a cost alert, this can go unnoticed until the monthly invoice.
Prompt as configuration:
the prompt template is a versioned artifact that can be rolled back independently of the application code. The on-call runbook must document this capability.
Model deprecation:
a pinned model ID can become unavailable with limited notice. The runbook must include a model downgrade path.
Pillar 1 — Monitoring and Alerting
Required dashboards
Every Claude feature that reaches production must have a monitoring dashboard covering the following metrics. The dashboard must be created before the feature ships, not during the first incident:
Metric
Panel type
Alert threshold
Who is paged
Request rate (RPM)
Time series
Alert if > 3× the 7-day baseline (sudden spike)
On-call engineer
Error rate (%)
Time series
Alert if > 2% over a 5-minute window
On-call engineer
P95 latency (ms)
Time series
Alert if > SLA target for 10 consecutive minutes
On-call engineer
Input tokens per request (avg)
Time series
Alert if > 120% of the p50 baseline for 15 minutes
Feature team (non-paging)
Output tokens per request (avg)
Time series
Alert if > 120% of the p50 baseline for 15 minutes
Feature team (non-paging)
Cost per hour (USD)
Time series + total
Alert if > $[team_threshold] / hour
Engineering manager + on-call
Rate limit hits (429 count)
Count
Alert if > 10 / minute
On-call engineer
stop_reason=max_tokens rate (%)
Time series
Alert if > 5% (suggests inputs growing beyond expectation)
Feature team (non-paging)

# Prometheus alert rules for a Claude feature
# Place in monitoring/alerts/claude-feature-alerts.yml

groups:
  - name: claude-invoice-assistant
    rules:
      - alert: ClaudeHighErrorRate
        expr: |
          rate(claude_api_requests_total{feature="invoice-assistant", status="error"}[5m])
          / rate(claude_api_requests_total{feature="invoice-assistant"}[5m]) > 0.02
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: page
          team: billing-eng
        annotations:
          summary: "Invoice assistant error rate > 2%"
          runbook: "https://wiki.acme.com/runbooks/claude-invoice-assistant"

      - alert: ClaudeHighCostRate
        expr: |
          sum(rate(claude_api_cost_usd_total{feature="invoice-assistant"}[1h])) * 3600 > 50
        for: 10m
        labels:
          severity: warning
          team: billing-eng
        annotations:
          summary: "Invoice assistant spending > $50/hour"
          description: "Check for token size regression or traffic spike"
Pillar 2 — Runbooks
Minimum runbook content
Every Claude feature must have a runbook linked from the monitoring dashboard and from the on-call rotation wiki page. The runbook must cover these scenarios:
High error rate:
distinguish Anthropic API errors (check status.anthropic.com) from application errors (check application logs). Include specific log query to identify the error type.
Rate limit errors (429):
identify which team tier is exhausted, check the current usage against the rate limit, and document the manual throttle switch location.
Cost spike:
query to find which feature/endpoint is driving the cost, how to identify if input token size grew, and the procedure to set an emergency spend cap.
Model unavailability:
steps to switch the model_id registry entry to the fallback model, how to confirm the switch took effect, and how to monitor the fallback model's performance.
Prompt rollback:
steps to revert the prompt template to the previous version, confirmation that the prior template version is tagged in git, and how to verify the rollback completed.
Truncated output complaints:
query to find stop_reason=max_tokens rate, how to identify which requests are truncating, temporary max_tokens bump procedure if applicable.

# Runbook: Invoice Assistant Claude Integration

## Quick links
- Dashboard: https://grafana.acme.com/d/claude-invoice
- Logs: https://kibana.acme.com/app/discover#claude-invoice
- Template registry: prompts/customer-facing/invoice-assistant.yml
- Prompt lead on-call: #prompt-oncall Slack channel

## Scenario: High error rate (>2%)

1. Check https://status.anthropic.com for active incidents.
   - If Anthropic is degraded: alert #incident-response and activate circuit breaker (see below).
   - If Anthropic is healthy: check application logs for the specific error class.

2. Query logs for error breakdown:
   ```
   index=claude-invoice status=error | stats count by error_type
   ```

3. If error_type is anthropic.RateLimitError:
   → See "Rate limit errors" scenario below.

4. If error_type is anthropic.APIStatusError with status=500:
   → Retry storm hitting Anthropic. Enable request queue with 2 RPS limit:
   ```
   kubectl set env deployment/invoice-assistant CLAUDE_MAX_RPS=2
   ```

## Scenario: Prompt rollback

1. Identify the last known-good version:
   ```
   git tag -l 'prompts/invoice-assistant/*' | sort -V | tail -5
   ```
2. Update the template registry to point to the prior version tag.
3. Restart the application pods to pick up the new registry value:
   ```
   kubectl rollout restart deployment/invoice-assistant
   ```
4. Confirm rollback: check dashboard for error rate returning to baseline.
   Expected recovery time: < 5 minutes after pod restart completes.
Pillar 3 — Capacity Planning
Rate limit budget allocation

Anthropic API rate limits are per-organisation (or per-project, depending on the tier). A new feature that uses significant token volume must be allocated a slice of the organisation's rate limit budget before shipping:

# rate-limit-budget.yml — organisation-level allocation
# Updated whenever a new Claude feature ships or traffic grows significantly.

total_tier_1_rpm: 10000     # Tier 1 requests per minute (organisation total)
total_input_tokens_per_min: 2000000

allocations:
  invoice-assistant:
    max_rpm: 2000
    max_input_tokens_per_min: 400000
    priority: high           # high = not throttled first under load
    cost_ceiling_usd_per_hour: 50

  support-chat:
    max_rpm: 3000
    max_input_tokens_per_min: 600000
    priority: high
    cost_ceiling_usd_per_hour: 75

  internal-tooling:
    max_rpm: 500
    max_input_tokens_per_min: 100000
    priority: low            # low = throttled first to protect customer-facing features
    cost_ceiling_usd_per_hour: 10

The operational readiness gate requires that the new feature's projected peak rate fits within the allocation. If it does not, the team must request a larger allocation or the feature cannot ship until the organisation's total rate limit is upgraded.
Pillar 4 — Rollback Plan
What rollback covers for Claude features
Unlike a traditional service rollback (revert code, redeploy), Claude feature rollbacks may need to revert any of three independently-versioned artifacts:
Artifact
How to roll back
Recovery time
Application code
Standard Kubernetes rollout undo or equivalent
< 3 minutes
Prompt template
Update registry to prior git tag; restart pods
< 5 minutes
Model ID
Update model registry entry to prior approved model; restart pods
< 5 minutes

The DoD requires that the rollback procedure for each artifact be documented, that the prior version of each artifact exists and is reachable (tagged in git, not only in HEAD), and that the estimated recovery time is < 15 minutes for any single-artifact rollback.
A rollback that requires a code deployment to change the prompt template is too slow. Prompt rollbacks must be achievable via configuration change without a code deployment.
Pillar 5 — Support Documentation
On-call FAQ
The on-call engineer encountering a Claude feature for the first time needs context that is not in the code. The FAQ must answer:
What does this feature do, in one sentence?
What are the known limitations the model cannot handle? (e.g., 'Cannot access real-time data; only answers from injected invoice context')
What does a correctly-functioning response look like vs an error state?
What user-visible error messages should the on-call engineer expect, and do they indicate application failure or expected graceful degradation?
Which team owns this feature? Who is the prompt lead?
When was the current prompt version deployed? What changed from the previous version?
Known limitations documentation
Every production prompt has limitations. Documenting them before shipping prevents false incident reports when the model behaves as designed rather than being broken:

# Known Limitations — Invoice Assistant

## Expected behaviours that may look like bugs

**Refuses to discuss competitor pricing**
This is by design. The system prompt instructs the model not to compare ACME prices
with competitors. User-facing message: "I can only help with ACME invoices and billing."

**Occasional language-switching on multilingual inputs**
The model may respond in the user's language even when the account language is set to
English. This is non-critical; the invoice data is always rendered correctly.
Not a paging condition.

**Truncated responses for very long invoices**
Invoices with >100 line items may trigger stop_reason=max_tokens. The model will
truncate its response mid-sentence. Application code shows a "View full invoice" link
in this case. If the rate exceeds 5%, alert the feature team — it may indicate
a context injection bug.

## Out of scope (by design, not a bug)
- Cannot access invoices older than 24 months (data not injected into context).
- Cannot process refunds, credits, or payment arrangements.
- Cannot answer questions about products not listed on the current invoice.
Operational Readiness Checklist
The following checklist is appended to the Definition of Done ticket. All items must be checked before the feature is marked operationally ready and eligible for production deployment:

## Operational Readiness — Claude Feature

### Monitoring
- [ ] Grafana/Datadog dashboard created and linked in team wiki
- [ ] Error rate alert configured (>2% over 5 min → page)
- [ ] P95 latency alert configured (>SLA target for 10 min → page)
- [ ] Cost per hour alert configured (>$[threshold] → page EM)
- [ ] Rate limit (429) alert configured (>10/min → page)
- [ ] Token size regression alert configured (>120% baseline → notify)
- [ ] stop_reason=max_tokens rate alert configured (>5% → notify)
- [ ] Dashboard linked from on-call wiki page

### Runbooks
- [ ] Runbook page created in wiki
- [ ] High error rate scenario documented
- [ ] Rate limit scenario documented
- [ ] Cost spike scenario documented
- [ ] Model unavailability + downgrade documented
- [ ] Prompt rollback steps documented (< 15 min RTO)
- [ ] Truncated output scenario documented
- [ ] Runbook URL added to all Prometheus/alert annotations

### Capacity
- [ ] Projected peak RPM documented and within rate limit allocation
- [ ] Projected peak token/min documented and within allocation
- [ ] Rate limit budget allocation YAML updated with this feature
- [ ] Cost ceiling set and agreed with EM

### Rollback Plan
- [ ] Prior prompt template version tagged in git
- [ ] Prompt rollback procedure documented and tested in staging
- [ ] Model downgrade path documented (fallback model specified in template)
- [ ] Code rollback procedure is standard (no special steps)
- [ ] All rollback paths estimated < 15 min

### Support Documentation
- [ ] One-sentence feature description written for on-call FAQ
- [ ] Known limitations documented (behaviours that look like bugs)
- [ ] User-visible error messages documented
- [ ] Feature owner and prompt lead contact documented
- [ ] Current prompt version and last deploy date recorded
Chaos Engineering for Claude Integrations
Why chaos testing applies to Claude features

Runbooks describe what the on-call engineer should do. Chaos tests verify that the application actually behaves correctly when those failure modes occur. Running a small set of targeted chaos scenarios in staging before production deployment provides much stronger operational confidence than documentation alone:
Chaos scenario
How to inject
Expected application behaviour
What fails if unprepared
Anthropic API returns 429 on every request
Intercept at the HTTP layer with a proxy returning 429
Circuit breaker opens; requests queued or rejected gracefully; user sees a retry message
Uncaught RateLimitError propagates to user as a 500; no retry message
Anthropic API latency spikes to 30s
Add artificial 30s delay to the proxy
Request times out after the configured timeout; fallback response served; alert fires on P95
User waits indefinitely; no timeout; alert does not fire because the request is still in-flight
Anthropic API returns 500 on every request
Proxy returns 500
After max_retries attempts, application returns a graceful error; fallback (cached or static) response used if configured
After retries, unhandled exception propagates
Prompt template registry returns the wrong version
Temporarily corrupt the registry entry to point to a non-existent tag
Application falls back to the last loaded version; alert fires; rollback procedure is invoked
Application crashes on startup; no fallback; RTO exceeds 15 min
Token count suddenly 3× baseline (simulated long input)
Inject a context block of ~3× normal size into all requests
Cost alert fires within 10 min; stop_reason=max_tokens alert fires; feature team notified
Cost spike goes undetected until the monthly bill; truncated responses create user complaints

# tests/chaos/test_rate_limit_chaos.py
# Run in staging with a mock proxy that injects 429 responses.
import pytest, asyncio, httpx
from unittest.mock import AsyncMock, patch

@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_rate_limit_triggers_graceful_degradation():
    """When Claude returns 429, the application returns a user-friendly message,
    does not expose the raw API error, and the circuit breaker eventually opens."""
    from myapp.invoice_assistant import handle_invoice_query

    # Patch the SDK to always raise RateLimitError
    rate_limit_error = __import__(
        'anthropic', fromlist=['RateLimitError']
    ).RateLimitError(
        message="Rate limit exceeded",
        response=AsyncMock(status_code=429, headers={}),
        body={},
    )

    with patch('anthropic.AsyncAnthropic') as mock_client_cls:
        mock_client = AsyncMock()
        mock_client.messages.create.side_effect = rate_limit_error
        mock_client_cls.return_value = mock_client

        response = await handle_invoice_query(
            user_id="user-123",
            account_id="ACC-00291",
            user_message="What are my charges?",
        )

    # Should not expose the raw error
    assert "RateLimitError" not in response.message
    assert "429" not in response.message
    # Should give a user-friendly message
    assert any(w in response.message.lower() for w in ["try again", "unavailable", "moment"])
    # HTTP status should be 503, not 500
    assert response.status_code == 503
Post-Launch Operational Review
First week review

After a new Claude feature ships to production (even at canary), an operational review should be scheduled for 5-7 business days later. The review checks that the monitoring and runbook are working as designed:
Alert fire rate:
did any alerts fire in the first week? Were they actionable or noisy? Tune thresholds if > 20% of alerts were noise (resolved without human action).
Runbook usage:
did the on-call engineer use the runbook for any incident? Was the information accurate and sufficient? Update immediately if any step was missing or wrong.
Actual vs projected cost:
compare actual token spend in the first week against the projection used in capacity planning. If > 20% variance, update the projection and re-check the cost ceiling.
Actual vs projected latency:
compare production P95 latency against the staging eval measurement. If production is significantly higher, investigate whether the production input distribution differs from the golden dataset.
stop_reason=max_tokens rate:
if the truncation rate is unexpectedly high, the context injection may be larger than expected in production. Adjust max_tokens or the context injection strategy.
The post-launch review is not optional. A feature that ships with incorrect runbooks or misconfigured alerts is operationally incomplete, regardless of whether an incident occurred in the first week.
Summary

Operational readiness is the fifth and final gate before a Claude feature is marked Done. It ensures the feature can be monitored, diagnosed, and recovered by any on-call engineer — not just its authors. The five pillars cover the distinct failure modes of Claude integrations: non-deterministic behaviour (monitoring + dashboards), external API dependency (runbooks with Anthropic status checks), token cost as an operational variable (capacity planning + cost alerts), independent prompt versioning (rollback plan covering code, prompt, and model separately), and non-obvious behaviour that looks like bugs (support documentation and known limitations).
Monitoring dashboards and alerts are configured before the feature ships, not during the first incident.
The runbook covers six scenarios: high error rate, rate limits, cost spike, model unavailability, prompt rollback, and truncated output.
Rate limit budget allocation is documented and the feature's projected peak rate fits within it.
Rollback is achievable in < 15 minutes for each independently-versioned artifact (code, prompt, model).
Known limitations are documented so on-call engineers can distinguish expected behaviour from genuine incidents.
